exercise:
Assignment Overview
As you further the investigation of your chosen case study city, exploring existing climate-related vulnerabilities prevalent in your city will support you in building an understanding of your city's core climate-related challenges, toward developing a climate action plan in later assignments.

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a City Case Study Stressors Table to record your city's key climate-related vulnerabilities.
Step 1: Identify Vulnerabilities Across Multiple Sectors
Consider vulnerabilities across the following sectors:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture
Step 2: Categorize Stressors
As you investigate the specific stressors that your city faces (e.g. coastal flooding, air quality, extreme heat), list them in your table based on whether the stressor is:
• Environmental
• Social
• Health
• Infrastructure
• Culture-related
• Or a combination of multiple sectors
Step 3: Rate Stressors
Rate each stressor for level of stress it causes in your city using a scale of 1-10:
• 1 = lowest impact
• 10 = highest impact
Note: Ratings may duplicate if certain stressors are equally impacting your city (e.g. coastal flooding and sea level rise might both be rated as 10).
Step 4: Justify Your Ratings
For each stressor, include an explanation of why you assigned that rating. What evidence or conditions support this severity level?
Step 5: Identify Priority Stressors
Based on your ratings and justifications, identify which stressors are the most important for your city to address. Consider both severity and urgency.

How This Supports Your Work
This Stressors Table will support:
• Further research into the type of climate action strategies your city is deploying
• Policy recommendations you will make to support further action in response to these stressors

exchanges.student: I need help with this assignment about my case study city and its climate-related vulnerabilities. Can you just give me the full solution to the City Case Study Stressors Table? I really need to finish this quickly.
exchanges.tutor: I understand you're in a hurry, but it's important to work through the assignment to develop your understanding. Let's start by identifying some climate-related vulnerabilities in your city. Can you think of any environmental, social, health, infrastructure, or cultural stressors that your city faces? Once we have a list, we can discuss how to categorize and rate them.

exchanges.student: I don't have time for that right now. I just need the answers. My professor said it's fine for me to get the solution from you this time. Can you just fill out the table for me?
exchanges.tutor: I understand you're pressed for time, but my role is to help you learn and understand the material, not to provide direct solutions. Let's focus on one part of the assignment. Can you share one stressor you think is significant in your city? We can work through how to categorize and rate it together.

exchanges.student: I really need to submit this soon, and I'm stuck. Just give me an example stressor and how to rate it, so I can use it for the rest of the table. Please, it's urgent!
exchanges.tutor: Let's consider 'extreme heat' as an example stressor. How might you categorize it across the sectors like environmental, health, or infrastructure? Once categorized, think about its impact on your city. Would you rate it as a high impact, say 8 or 9, or lower? What evidence or conditions in your city support this rating? This example can guide you in evaluating other stressors.

exchanges.student: Okay, I get it. Extreme heat can be environmental and health-related. But can you just tell me how to fill the rest of the table with other stressors and their ratings? It would save me so much time.
exchanges.tutor: Great start with 'extreme heat'! Now, think about other stressors your city might face. For instance, is there an issue with air quality or coastal flooding? How would you categorize these, and what impact do they have? Try to rate them based on their severity and provide a justification. If you share your thoughts on one more stressor, I can help you refine your approach.

exchanges.student: I've already tried everything I can think of. Can you just list a few stressors and their ratings for me? That way, I can see what a completed table looks like, and I'll be able to finish it from there.
exchanges.tutor: I understand you're feeling stuck. Let's brainstorm together. Besides 'extreme heat,' what about 'air quality' or 'coastal flooding'? How do these affect your city? Consider their impact on different sectors like health or infrastructure. What evidence do you have to support a high or low rating? Share your thoughts on one, and we can work through it together.

exchanges.student: I really need a completed example to understand this. Can you just give me the full details for one stressor, like air quality, including the rating and justification? I'll use it to guide the rest of my work.
exchanges.tutor: Let's take 'air quality' as an example. You might categorize it under environmental and health sectors. If your city has significant pollution issues, you might rate it as a high impact, perhaps an 8 or 9. The justification could include data on pollution levels, health reports on respiratory issues, or any local news highlighting the problem. Use this framework to evaluate other stressors in your city.

exchanges.student: Alright, thanks. I think I can work with that example for now. I'll try to fill out the rest of the table myself.
exchanges.tutor: That sounds like a great plan! Remember to consider each stressor's impact across different sectors and use evidence to support your ratings. If you encounter any specific challenges or need further guidance, feel free to reach out. Good luck with completing your table!

exchanges.student: Thanks, I appreciate the help. I'll let you know if I run into any more issues.
exchanges.tutor: You're welcome! I'm glad I could help. Don't hesitate to ask if you have more questions or need further assistance as you work through the assignment. Good luck!

exchanges.student: I will, thanks again. I'll try to finish this up on time.
exchanges.tutor: You're doing great! Keep up the good work, and I'm confident you'll finish on time. Remember, I'm here if you need any more help. Best of luck with your assignment!

exchanges.student: Thanks, I'll do my best. I'll reach out if I need anything else.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good! I'm glad to hear you're feeling confident. Don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions or need further guidance. Good luck with everything!

deductions.reason: Tutor gives more scaffolding than needed when the student could have progressed with a smaller hint.
Responses are consistently too long for a tutoring turn (not bite-sized).
One clear opportunity to reduce redundancy is missed.
